Okay, onboarding welcome email. So I've provided you with a template for this email as well as the next, but I just wanted to briefly provide you with context as to what the purpose is. So we just talked about the general onboarding and what it looks like in segments, right? So the welcome email. This is going to serve as the overview of what they should expect next, as well as the nitty gritty paperwork. Meaning, this is what they get after they've accepted the job offer. And it's going to tell them all the things that they need to fill out in order to officially become an employee of your company. Okay, so this is not talking about their onboarding. It's just letting them know what's happening right now and then saying at the end of the email, hey, you'll be getting your next email in X amount of days. Here's the point of contact, you know, for emails, questions, whatever, going forward until you get that next one. Okay. And so it's just, it is a point of communication very soon after bringing someone on. So that's why it's important that you be thorough during it. The example I provide you is incredibly thorough. So I recommend that you work off that and use it well. From there, after you have gotten back all their initial paperwork and your two to three days prior to onboarding them, you want to send this email and this onboarding email is their first day and week onboarding email. And so this basically serves as an overview of what they should expect arriving their first day, as well as the agenda for the week ahead at minimum. Okay, and so a lot of people get really nervous going into a new job. And so what you're doing is you're alleviating them of that nervousness, right? And you're calming their anxieties, you're telling them what to expect, you're saying, hey, maybe you need to bring this, wear this, expect this. Okay, and so people are scared of what they don't know. So you want to calm their fears by giving them a very clear outline of what's about to happen next. Okay.
